chunk_summary: The convergence model of India's national nutrition programs integrates governance, delivery platforms, interventions, and data systems to address pediatric nutrition, focusing on under-6 services, school meals, and health platforms while emphasizing clinical verification of digital records.
learning_objectives: Understand the four-layer convergence model of India's national nutrition programs.
Identify key pediatric nutrition delivery platforms and their roles.
Analyze the gaps between reported service coverage and clinical outcomes using NFHS-5 data.
Differentiate between nutrition-specific and nutrition-sensitive interventions in clinical practice.
key_facts: India's under-five stunting rate is 35.5%, wasting is 19.3%, and anaemia prevalence is 67.1% (NFHS-5).
99.2% of households report iodized salt coverage, but clinical outcomes show persistent malnutrition risks.
Pediatric nutrition services operate through Anganwadi (ICDS), NHM health platforms, school programs (PM POSHAN), and food-system regulation (FSSAI).
Saksham Anganwadi and POSHAN 2.0 focus on under-6 nutrition and early childhood services.
Digital records are proxies for service delivery but require clinical verification using growth charts, dose dates, and diet history.
reference_quote: NFHS-5 highlights why: under-five stunting is 35.5%, wasting 19.3%, and anaemia 67.1% even while iodized salt coverage is reported at 99.2%, so 'benefits issued' does not equal 'risk resolved.'
document_key_topics: Convergence architecture of national nutrition programs
Pediatric nutrition delivery platforms and interventions
Clinical verification of digital service records
NFHS-5 data on malnutrition and service gaps
